h1: Calculate Your Home's Monthly Electricity Costs

intro: Calculate your home's monthly electricity consumption and costs with precision. Understanding kilowatt-hour (kWh) usage and device wattage helps you identify energy-hungry appliances and reduce your utility bills. This 2026 calculator uses standard electrical formulas and is updated regularly to keep you informed.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **kWh/month = W × hours/day × 30 / 1000**. Enter your appliance's wattage, daily usage hours, and electricity rate to calculate monthly consumption and cost.

Homeowners seeking to reduce energy bills and identify power hogs

fields.label: Hours per Day
fields.help: Average hours the appliance runs daily.

explanation:
## How We Calculate

This calculator uses the standard formula for estimating monthly electricity consumption:

**kWh/month = (Power in Watts × Hours per day × 30 days) / 1000**

The calculator applies your input values and validates them against typical ranges for common household appliances.

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide only. For critical decisions (budgeting, equipment replacement, energy audits), consult with a qualified professional. Values are current for 2026 and updated periodically.

faq.q: How much does it cost to run a refrigerator monthly?
faq.a: A typical refrigerator uses 400–800 kWh/year. At $0.12/kWh, that's $48–$96 annually, or $4–$8/month.

faq.q: Which home appliances use the most electricity?
faq.a: Air conditioning (1,000–3,000W), water heaters, refrigerators, and electric ovens are the biggest energy consumers. AC can dominate summer bills.

faq.q: What's the difference between watts and kilowatts?
faq.a: One kilowatt (kW) = 1,000 watts (W). Monthly bills measure kilowatt-hours (kWh): energy consumed over time. A 100W device running 10 hours uses 1 kWh.

faq.q: How do I calculate my electricity bill?
faq.a: Multiply your total monthly kWh usage by your utility's rate per kWh. This calculator helps you estimate consumption for individual appliances.

faq.q: Can I save money by unplugging appliances?
faq.a: Yes. Standby power (phantom load) from plugged-in devices costs 1–10W each. Unplugging chargers and turning off real power switches saves money over time.

faq.q: What does standby power consumption mean?
faq.a: Standby power is energy used when a device is off but plugged in—like TVs, monitors, and cable boxes. It can add 5–10% to your bill.
Yes, it uses standard electrical formulas validated internationally. However, real consumption varies by appliance age and usage patterns—use this as a reference guide.

faq.q: Do you save my data or calculations?
faq.a: No. All calculations happen in your browser. We don't send your data to any server or store it.

faq.q: How can I reduce my electricity costs?
faq.a: Switch to LED bulbs (80% less than incandescent), unplug devices on standby, use fans instead of AC when possible, and run large loads during off-peak hours.

faq.q: What's the average US household electricity rate?
faq.a: As of 2026, average US residential rates range from $0.10–$0.20/kWh, varying by state and utility. Check your bill for your exact rate.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
